content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November 22,
1994
Daniel J.
McHugh
City Attorney
City of Redlands
P.O.
Box 3005
Redlands,
CA
92373—1505
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-94—347
Dear Mr.
McHugh:
This letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding the duties and responsibilities of City of Redlands
Councilmember William C.
Cunningham under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
Please note that our advice is limited to the provisions of
the Act.
In addition,
nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
Also,
this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The Commission
does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re
Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
OUESTION
Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require
Councilmember Cunningham to disqualify himself from participating
in city decisions regarding the adoption of a city ordinance on
composting operations within the city?
CONCLUSION
Councilmember Cunningham does not have a financial interest
in The Redlands Association.
Thus, the conflict—of—interest
provisions of the Act do not require him to disqualify himself
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000
- 18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-94--347
Page
2
from participating in any city decisions affecting The Redlands
V
Association, including decisions concerning the proposed ordinance
and litigation which may arise if the ordinance is adopted by the
city.
FACTS
The City of Redlands has entered into a Settlement Agreement
and Mutual General Release with Larry Curti and
others regarding a
dispute over Mr. Curti’s coiuposting operations.
As part of the
settlement agreement, the city agreed to initiate, within 120
days, the necessary general plan and zoning code amenthnents to
include the composting operations, fertilizer,
and landscape
supply sales as permitted, approved uses within the land use and
zone designated for the Curti property.
To comply with a portion
of the settlement agreement, the city has drafted an ordinance
which would allow composting operations within the city’s
agricultural land use designations.
The Redlands Association,
a nonprofit organization which
routinely makes presentations to the city on specific issues such
as land use recently made a presentation to the city council
questioning the legality of adopting the proposed ordinance.
Councilmember Cunningham is president of The Redlands Association.
He receives no monetary stipend or other consideration for serving
as president of this organization.
The Redlands Association owns
no property.
It has an open membership and an elected board.
The
organization is supported by dues from members.
Members,
however,
have no obligation for any debts incurred by the organization.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in making,
or otherwise using their official
position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a
financial interest.
Councilmember Cunningham is a public
official.
(Section 82048.)
V
Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a governmental decision, within the meaning of Section
87100, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally,
on the official,
or on a member of the
official’s immediate family
2 or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
****
V
2
An official’s “immediate family” are his or her spouse and
dependent children.
(Section 82029.)


Our File No.
A-94--347
Page
3
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
Section 87103(a),
(c)
and (d).
Accordingly,
Councilmember Cunningham may not make,
participate in making, or attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision if the decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
councilmember,
or on a member of his immediate family,
or on any
of his economic interests, as specified above.
You have advised us that Councilmember Cunningham is
president of The Redlands Association.
According to your facts,
The Redlands Association is a nonprofit organization.
The
definition of “business entity” in the Act excludes nonprofit
organizations.
(Section 82005.)
Thus,
The Redlands Association
does not constitute an economic interest of Councilinember
Cunningham under either Section 87103(a)
or Section 87103(d).
Furthermore,
since you have indicated that the councilmember
has not received any monetary stipend or other consideration for
serving as president of the organization, he has not received any
income from the organization.
Therefore,
Councilmember Cunningham
does not have a financial interest in The Redlands Association
under Section 87103(c), and he is not prohibited from
participating in any city decisions that affect The Redlands
Association.
This would include decisions concerning the proposed
ordinance or litigation that may arise if the city adopts the
ordinance.
If, however,
Counci1member Cunningham should receive income
3
from the nonprofit organization of more than $250, he should
Please note,
however, that pursuant to Section 82030(b) (2)
income does not include reimbursement for travel expenses and per
diem received from a bona fide educational,
academic,
or
charitable organization.


Our File No.
A-94—347
Page
4
refer to Commission Regulations 18702.1 and 18702.5 to determine
whether a decision would have a material financial effect on the
organization, or he may contact us for further advice.
Copies of
the regulations are enclosed.
We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter,
do
not hesitate to call me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0c per page.
